title: Accelerating Eigenvalue Dataset Generation via Chebyshev Subspace Filter

reviews.summary: This paper proposes SCSF, a two-stage pipeline to accelerate the generation of labeled eigenvalue datasets for PDE operators by exploiting similarities across problem instances. First, a truncated FFT-based greedy sort orders operators so that spectrally similar problems are adjacent. Second, a Chebyshev-filtered subspace iteration (ChFSI) solves each problem by reusing the previous problem’s Ritz pairs to initialize the invariant subspace and estimate spectral bounds. Experiments on Poisson, second-order elliptic, and Helmholtz operators report up to 6× end-to-end speedups over standard Lanczos/Krylov/Davidson solvers, with ablations showing the benefit of sorting and subspace reuse.
strengths: Leverages an inexpensive, truncated-FFT signature of parameter fields to arrange a sequence of similar eigenproblems, which is simple and practical.

Integrates sequence-aware initialization with a Chebyshev-filtered subspace iteration to recycle subspaces across instances, an idea known in electronic-structure workflows but here applied to dataset generation with explicit sorting to enhance correlation.
Evaluates across three PDE operator families and varies both number of requested eigenpairs and matrix dimensions.
Includes ablations on sorting vs. no sorting, and on initializing baselines with previous eigenvectors, providing insight into where gains originate.
Provides time breakouts for sorting and discusses complexity.
Overall pipeline and algorithms are described step-by-step, with pseudocode for the Chebyshev filter and the ChFSI loop.
The problem setup (discretized Hermitian PDE operators) and the goal (accelerated dataset generation) are clearly motivated.
Addresses a real bottleneck in building training datasets for neural operator/eigenvalue surrogates: generating many closely related eigenproblems efficiently.
Demonstrates that modest method engineering (ordering plus warm-started ChFSI) can substantially cut time-to-label in standard PDE cases.
weaknesses: The sequential-warm-start strategy reduces across-sample parallelism—an important consideration for dataset generation in HPC settings—yet the paper does not analyze throughput trade-offs or recommend hybrid parallelization strategies.

The method and analysis are restricted to standard Hermitian problems; many PDE discretizations yield generalized Hermitian (Ax=λBx), non-Hermitian, or interior-spectrum targets. How SCSF extends to those is not discussed.

Chebyshev filtering details (interval selection, mapping to amplify the desired spectral slice for “smallest by magnitude” targets) are under-specified; correctness and robustness depend on these choices.

Baselines omit modern, sequence-aware or polynomial-filtered solvers explicitly designed to reuse subspaces, such as CHASE/ChASE-GPU and EVSL-PSI, as well as recent residual-based ChFSI variants. These are the most relevant comparators and could diminish the reported advantage.

Tuning of existing baselines is minimal (defaults); state-of-the-art implementations (e.g., KS/JD with appropriate subspace sizing/preconditioning or EVSL polynomial filters) are not explored, which weakens the fairness of comparisons.

Accuracy and convergence criteria are not consistently audited; while a target precision is reported, there is no systematic residual or eigenspace error comparison across methods to ensure matched solution quality.

Some critical parameters in the Chebyshev filter (α, β selection and mapping strategy for “smallest |λ|”) are described informally; the role of λ, c, e in Algorithm 1 would benefit from a precise derivation and a reproducible recipe.
The complexity discussion for sorting assumes k ≪ p and p ≪ N; while k ≪ p is reasonable, p ≪ N is not always true, and the N^2 greedy phase will not scale to very large datasets (N ≳ 10^5).

There is rich prior work on (i) Chebyshev-filtered subspace iteration for sequences (e.g., Winkelmann et al. 2019; Berljafa et al. 2015; ChASE-GPU 2022), (ii) adaptive polynomial filtering (e.g., AdaPolySI 2026), and (iii) alternative accelerated workflows for eigenproblems in sequences (e.g., PDESHEP). These are cited in part but not engaged experimentally.

Recent approaches that accelerate eigenvalue workflows with learned spectral priors (e.g., DeepContour) show an alternative route to reduce scouting and should be discussed to refine the “first to accelerate eigenvalue data generation” claim.
reviews.questions:
1. How are α and β (Chebyshev interval bounds) chosen in practice for targeting the smallest-in-magnitude eigenvalues, and how do you ensure robust amplification for interior vs. extremal targets across the sequence? Please provide a reproducible recipe and sensitivity analysis.
2. Why were CHASE/EVSL (and, if possible, AdaPolySI) not included as baselines? Can you add experiments comparing SCSF to these sequence-aware polynomial-filter methods with warm starts?
3. What residual tolerances and stopping criteria were enforced across all solvers, and how do the achieved residual norms/eigenspace errors compare? Please include accuracy audits to ensure matched solution quality.
4. How does SCSF handle generalized Hermitian definite problems (Ax = λBx), which are common in PDE discretizations? Can your approach reuse prior (Λ, V) effectively with Chebyshev filtering in that setting?
5. For large datasets (N ≳ 10^5), the O(N^2) greedy step will dominate. Could you discuss approximate or hierarchical sorts (e.g., k‑NN graphs on truncated-FFT embeddings, space-filling curves) and report preliminary scaling results?
6. Can you comment on parallelization strategies for dataset generation when sequential warm-starting restricts concurrency? For example, partitioning into multiple correlated sequences or pipelining groups—how does this affect end-to-end throughput?
7. The Helmholtz operator can be indefinite; under which discretization/boundary setups is your matrix Hermitian, and are there cases where non-Hermitian effects reduce SCSF effectiveness?
reviews.assessment: The paper addresses an important, practical bottleneck—fast generation of eigenvalue labels for ML—by combining a sensible ordering heuristic (truncated FFT on parameters) with sequence-aware Chebyshev-filtered subspace iteration. The approach is conceptually sound and the empirical results, including ablations, are promising. However, the current evaluation omits the most relevant baselines (CHASE/EVSL/adaptive polynomial filtering), provides limited detail on Chebyshev parameterization for interior/smallest‑magnitude targets, and explores only modest matrix sizes with minimal accuracy auditing and no discussion of parallel-throughput trade-offs. The contribution is likely incremental relative to established sequence-aware Chebyshev methods, with the main novelty being the simple FFT-based sorting tailored to dataset generation. With stronger baselines, clearer accuracy controls, and a discussion of scaling/parallelization, the work could be valuable to the ICML community interested in scientific ML data curation. As it stands, I view it as a solid idea with incomplete validation for a top-tier venue.
